text: Catalytic conversion of carbon dioxide (CO 2 ) into fuels and chemicals such as methanol (CH 3 OH) is considered as a viable method for utilizing the captured CO 2 . In this study, we have investigated the activity of size selected Cu 4 clusters supported on alumina thin film for the reduction of CO 2 to CH 3 OH under near-atmospheric pressure and low CO 2 concentration. The oxidation state of the clusters was probed in situ using grazing incidence X-ray absorption spectroscopy (GI-XAS). Cu 4 clusters showed unprecedented activity for the conversion of CO 2 into CH 3 OH at low pressure. Density functional theory (DFT) studies indicated that Cu 4 clusters facilitated a lower activation energy barrier for the formation of CH 3 OH. Sub-nanometer copper clusters hence hold a great promise as an efficient catalyst for the catalytic conversion of CO 2 into value-added fuels and chemicals.
